steps.n: This is the basic form of the word that indicates time. It usually refers to past events.

steps.m: when (referring to future events, introducing 'ٱنشَقَّتْ' - splits apart)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end changes the time reference from past to future. In this ayah, it introduces what will happen when the sky splits apart.
The root letters convey the basic meaning of height or elevation
Transforms the root meaning of 'rising up' into the concrete noun 'sky'
Makes the noun definite
Doubles the middle letter to intensify meaning
Shows this word is the subject of the sentence
The root letters express the basic meaning of splitting or breaking
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: it split by itself (subject: السَّمَآءُ - the sky)
steps.n: Added ان at the beginning to show the sky splits on its own, without someone splitting it
Added ت because السماء (sky) is feminine in Arabic
Added ٱ (hamzat al-wasl) which helps pronounce the ن at the start
Changed from root 'to allow/listen' into past tense 'he/it listened/obeyed' (not she)
Adding ت at the end makes it feminine since 'sky' (السماء) is feminine in Arabic
Adding و at the start connects this action to what came before in the surah
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone's permanent role or quality, not just a temporary action
ها at the end shows this belongs to something feminine (in this case, the sky/heaven mentioned in the ayah). The kasra under ب shows it follows the preposition لِ.

steps.m: to/for her nurturer/sustainer (possessed by the sky/heaven)
steps.n: لِ at the start shows direction or purpose - here it shows the sky is submitting/listening to its Lord
Added shadda to show emphasis of the middle letter
Changed the first vowel to show something was done to the subject rather than the subject doing something
Added feminine marker to show the subject is feminine (referring back to السماء/heaven in context)

steps.m: and she/it was made true/proper (connected to أَذِنَتْ/obeyed)
steps.n: Added و at the start to connect this word with the previous word أَذِنَتْ (obeyed) in the ayah

steps.m: when (introduces what happens at a specific time)
steps.n: This is a word that introduces something that happens at a specific time, like saying "at the time when" in English
The و (and) connects this phrase to what came before it
The root meaning becomes a concrete noun referring to the earth or ground
Added vowels (َ and ْ) to the root letters to make the word 'earth/land'

steps.m: earth (as the doer of مُدَّتْ/was spread out)
steps.n: The ُ shows that 'earth' is doing the action of being spread out in this ayah

steps.m: the earth (as the doer of مُدَّتْ/was spread out)
steps.n: Added ال to specify we're talking about THE earth, not just any land
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive form by adding damma at start and combining the double د with shadda
Added ت to show this happened to something feminine (the earth/الأرض)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changes from 'to throw' to 'to make something be thrown' by adding أ at the start. Like changing from doing it yourself to making it happen.
Changed from 'he' to 'she' by adding ت at the end
Added و at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it
This is a relative pronoun that connects to فِيهَا (in it) to ask about what is contained inside
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Basic preposition meaning 'in' - one of the most common Arabic words that doesn't come from a root
Added ها which refers back to الأَرْضُ (the earth) mentioned earlier. This shows what is being thrown into.
Changed from root to past tense by adding vowels (fatha) and ending with alif
Doubled the middle letter to show the action is done thoroughly
Added تَ at the beginning to show the action happens to itself
Added تْ to show a feminine subject did this in the past

steps.m: and it (feminine) emptied itself completely (connected to وَأَلْقَتْ)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this action with the previous word وَأَلْقَتْ (and it cast out)
Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he listened' or 'he permitted'
Adding تْ at the end makes it feminine. This word is connected to حُقَّتْ (it was worthy) in this ayah.
Adding و at the start means 'and'
The shadda (ّ) on ب shows emphasis, making it mean someone who nurtures/sustains completely
Added هَا at the end to show this lord belongs to or is connected to something feminine (in this case, the earth from the previous verse)
Added لِ at beginning to show this is what the earth listened/responded to. The earth listened TO its Lord.
Changed to passive voice - something was made true by someone else
The ت shows this is talking about something feminine (in this case, referring back to the sky/heaven)

steps.m: and it (feminine) was made true/proper (referring to السماء/the sky)
steps.n: Adding و at the start connects this to the previous statement about the sky listening to its Lord
Adding damma to show this word is the subject
The word 'ya' (يا) was added at the start to call out to someone, like saying 'Hey!' or 'O!'
Added ها at the end to make it address someone specifically, like saying 'hey you!'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'human' - humans are social beings who are close/familiar with each other
Added damma to show this word is the subject of the sentence
Added 'al' to make it specific/definite - now talking about a particular human
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger or more emphasized, like adding an exclamation mark in English
Added كَ to show we're talking about 'you' (when speaking to one male). This word connects to كَادِحٌ (striving) to tell us who is doing the striving.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed the pattern to show who does the action by adding alif after first letter and kasra under second letter

steps.m: one who strives/works hard (addressing the human mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added double damma to show this describes an indefinite subject. This word is describing الْإِنسٰنُ (the human) mentioned earlier in the verse.
This is a basic preposition that shows movement or direction towards something. In this ayah, it connects 'striving' (كَادِحٌ) with 'your Lord' (رَبِّكَ) to show you are striving towards your Lord.
The small alif (ىٰ) at the end is just a writing style that doesn't change the meaning - it's still pronounced the same way
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic word form
The doubled middle letter (shadda) combines the repeated letters and intensifies the meaning to show complete nurturing authority
Added kasra to show this word is being possessed by something
Added كَ to show it belongs to 'you'. This word follows إِلَىٰ (to) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb

steps.m: striving hard (emphasizing the action of كَادِحٌ)
steps.n: Changed to a verbal noun that emphasizes the action. This word emphasizes the striving mentioned by كَادِحٌ (one who strives) in this ayah, showing how the person is definitely striving toward their Lord.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he met' to 'one who meets' by adding مُ at the start and ي at the end
Added هِ to show who is being met. This word connects to كَادِحٌ (striving) in the ayah, showing who will do the meeting.
Added the conjunction فَ (fa) at the beginning to mean 'and then'
This is a common particle used to introduce a condition or topic. The shadda (ّ) shows emphasis.

steps.m: then as for (introduces the first part of مَنْ أُوتِىَ - whoever is given)
steps.n: The فَ prefix shows this is connected to what comes next. In this ayah, it introduces the first part of a comparison about whoever is given their book in their right hand.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to the person who will be given their book.
Changed to passive form to show something was given to someone rather than someone bringing something
The weak letter ا changes to و after the passive voice vowel pattern
Added vowel marks to change from action to thing. The kasra (ِ) under first letter and fatha (َ) after second letter make it a noun.
This pattern is often used for things that result from the action of the root. The word كتاب is the object (what is being given) in this ayah.
The ه ('his') was added at the end to show it's his book. The word is what was given to him.
Added ُۥ at the end to show it belongs to him (like saying 'his')
Added vowels to make a noun meaning 'right side'
Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by something (shown by هِ in next step)
Added هِ to show it belongs to him

steps.m: by/with his right hand (connected to أُوتِىَ كِتٰبَهُۥ - given his book)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to show how something was done. This word explains how the book was given in the phrase 'given his book'.

steps.m: in the future (shows يُحَاسَبُ will happen later)
steps.n: This is a particle that shows future tense, similar to 'will' in English. It indicates that the accounting (يُحَاسَبُ) will happen in the future.

steps.m: then in the future (shows يُحَاسَبُ will happen later)
steps.n: The فَ at the beginning shows this happens as a result of something before it. It connects the future accounting (يُحَاسَبُ) with what was mentioned before.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif makes it about two people counting/settling with each other

steps.m: he will be held accountable (main verb of فَسَوْفَ)
steps.n: Adding يُ at start and ُ at end makes it passive voice (something happening to someone) and present/future tense. This connects to فَسَوْفَ (then soon) which makes it definitely future tense.

steps.m: calculation (the act or result of counting)
steps.n: Added ا after س and changed the vowels to make it into a noun showing the process or result of calculating

steps.m: a reckoning (object of يُحَاسَبُ - will be brought to account)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb يُحَاسَبُ (will be brought to account). Like saying 'he will face a reckoning'.
Changed the root 'to make easy' into an adjective meaning 'easy' by adding vowels
Added ًا at the end to show this word is describing the reckoning (حِسَابًا) mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Adding ن makes it about doing the action to oneself - like turning oneself back

steps.m: and he returns/turns himself back (to أَهْلِهِ his family)
steps.n: Added و to connect this action with what came before. In this ayah, he's returning to his family.
Basic preposition showing direction or destination
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is a spelling variation of the long 'aa' sound
Added a small alif (ٓ) above to show connection to the next word أَهْلِهِۦ (his family). This preposition shows he is returning to his family.
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to something (in this case, connected to what comes after it)
Added هِۦ at the end to show this is his family. The ۦ mark helps with pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Added مَ at start and changed middle letter to show someone who is happy (like saying 'a happy person')

steps.m: happily (describing how he returns to his family)
steps.n: Added tanween fatHa to show this describes how he returns to his family in the phrase 'وَيَنقَلِبُ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا' (and he will return to his family happily)
This is a basic conditional particle that introduces a new topic or case

steps.m: and as for... (introduces the second case in وَأَمَّا مَنْ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this to a previous statement. This word introduces the second case being discussed in the ayah.

steps.m: whoever (referring to the person receiving their book in أُوتِىَ كِتٰبَهُ)
steps.n: This is a special word that introduces a new person or group being talked about. Here it refers to the person who will receive their book of deeds. It connects to the verb أُوتِىَ (is given) that comes after it.
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to someone rather than someone doing it
Changed spelling due to weak letter rules, but meaning stays the same. This word refers to being given one's book of deeds.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changes from a verb (wrote) to a noun (book) by adding alif after second letter
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being given in the phrase 'was given his book'
Added هُۥ at the end to show the book belongs to him
Added alif and hamza to make it a noun meaning 'behind'
Added fatha at end and lengthened the alif. This word relates to ظَهْرِهِۦ (his back) in the ayah, showing something is behind someone's back.

steps.m: to show up or be visible on the surface

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to appear' into noun meaning 'back'
Added ِ to show this back belongs to something (in this case, it belongs to the word 'behind')

steps.m: his back (being owned by وَرَآءَ - behind)
steps.n: Added هِۦ at the end to show the back belongs to 'him'
This is a common word that shows something will definitely happen in the future, like 'will' in English. The و with sukun (ْ) makes a long 'o' sound.
The فَ at the beginning shows this happens as a result of something before it. This word connects to يَدْعُوا۟ (call/invoke) that comes after it in the ayah.
This is the basic meaning of the root
The root letters got vowels (fathas) to make it past tense 'he called'. The last letter و changed to ا which often happens at the end of Arabic words.
Added يَ at start to show present/future tense. The middle letter gets sukun, and the last root letter gets damma. The weak letter و appears at the end.
Added ا with small circle at end to show multiple people doing the action
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to damma and added و to make it a noun meaning the concept of destruction

steps.m: destruction (as the object of يَدْعُوا۟ - he will call)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this word is receiving the action of calling in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adds يَ at the start to change from past (he burned) to present/future (he burns/will burn)

steps.m: and he will burn (in سَعِيرًا - blazing fire)
steps.n: The و at the beginning means 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
This form makes a noun that describes something with intense qualities
The ًا ending shows this is what's being entered/burned in. It connects to the verb يَصْلَىٰ (he will enter/burn in) by telling us what he'll enter.
This is a special word used at the start of sentences to make what follows stronger or more certain. It's one of the emphasis words in Arabic that doesn't come from a root.
Added هُۥ at the end to show who is being talked about. This word connects to كَانَ (was) in the ayah 'كَانَ فِىٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا' (he was happy among his family).
Added vowel marks (fathas) to change from the root meaning 'to be' into 'he was'
Changed from basic past tense to hollow verb form by adding alif
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it shows that someone was happy within/among his family.
The ي is written as ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top when it comes at the end of a word in the Quran. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to settle, to be familiar with

steps.n: Changed from a verb about settling to a noun meaning the people who live together
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something
Added هِۦ to show this belongs to him. This word comes after في (in/among) in the ayah.

steps.m: to be happy, to feel joy

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added مَ at the start and changed vowels to show someone who is made happy (passive participle)

steps.m: joyfully, in a happy state (describing his state in أَهْلِهِۦ)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to change it from 'happy' to 'happily/in a happy state' - shows how he returned to his family
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it

steps.m: indeed he (subject of ظَنَّ - he thought)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who we're talking about. This person is the one who does the action of thinking (ظَنَّ) in this verse.

steps.m: to think or assume something without being certain

steps.n: Added fatha marks to make it 'he' form in past tense - like saying 'he thought' instead of just 'to think'
Combined the double ن into one letter with shadda. This word shows what 'he' thought about يَحُورَ (returning).

steps.m: that (connects to what follows ظَنَّ/he thought)
steps.n: This is a basic connecting word that introduces what someone thought. In this ayah, it connects 'he thought' (ظَنَّ) with what he thought.

steps.m: that (connects to what follows ظَنَّ/he thought)
steps.n: Added a fatha mark (َ) on the أ to show how to pronounce it correctly
This is a basic negative word that makes future actions impossible
The ل gets doubled (shadda) because it comes after the word أَن, which is a common rule in Arabic
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, and changed middle vowel to ُ which is common for this type of verb

steps.m: he returns back (object of ظَنَّ - he thought)
steps.n: Changed the ending to َ because it comes after لَن (will never). This verb shows what was thought - it's the object of ظَنَّ (he thought).

Added fatha marks to show exact pronunciation. This word is used specifically to strongly agree with something that was stated in the negative. In this ayah, it's responding to the previous negative statement.
Added alif maksura (ى with small mark) at the end to show proper pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: yes indeed! (contradicting a negative, with emphasis)
steps.n: Added a small mark (ٓ) above the final letter to show emphasis in pronunciation. This adds strength to the contradiction.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'if' or 'whether'
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word. This emphasizes the phrase that follows it (رَبَّهُۥ - his Lord)

steps.m: he cared for and helped grow
steps.n: Added basic vowels to connect the letters together

steps.m: one who completely cares for and helps grow
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
Added هُۥ meaning 'his' at the end. The word is affected by إِنَّ which came before it in the verse, showing it's being acted upon.
Added basic past tense vowels to show this happened in the past

steps.m: he was (describing his state in بِهِۦ بَصِيرًا - with him All-Seeing)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense. This word connects to بِهِۦ بَصِيرًا showing that Allah was always All-Seeing of him.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition that connects to what comes after it
Added هِ (him/it) to show who/what the preposition connects to

steps.m: with him/it (referring back to رَبَّهُۥ - his Lord)
steps.n: Added small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This word refers back to 'his Lord' mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed the pattern to show someone who has this quality very strongly. This word describes كَانَ (was) in this ayah.

steps.m: one who sees deeply (as a description of كَانَ)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is describing something in the sentence (it's an adjective)
Added fatha to show clear pronunciation of the negation particle
Extended the alif to emphasize the negation. This is a stylistic choice in writing that shows the strength of the negation.

steps.m: Then no/not (strong negation of أُقْسِمُ - I swear)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this negation with what came before. This فَ means 'then' or 'so' and shows this negation is a result or response to something.

steps.m: to take an oath or swear

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Vowels changed from past tense (he swore) to present tense (swears) - notice the 'i' sound in the middle

steps.m: I swear (before بِٱلشَّفَقِ - by the twilight)
steps.n: Added أُ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action. This word is connected to what follows (بِٱلشَّفَقِ) which tells what is being sworn by.

steps.m: twilight glow, reddish horizon at sunset

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic noun
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ش with shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: by the twilight glow (object of أُقْسِمُ)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to show swearing by something, and kasra at end because it follows بِ. This word is what is being sworn by in the phrase 'I swear by (أُقْسِمُ) the twilight'
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a proper word
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'the night' instead of just any night. The ل gets a shadda because it combines with the ل from ال
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and', added ِ at the end because it's connected to what comes after it (وَمَا وَسَقَ - and what it envelops)
This is a particle word used to ask questions or connect ideas, like 'what' or 'that which'
Added 'وَ' at the beginning to mean 'and'

steps.m: to gather or collect things together

steps.n: The basic past tense verb form is now complete
Root letters get fatha vowels to make the simple noun 'moon' from the idea of moonlight shining
Adds the definite article 'the' to specify we're talking about THE moon
Adds kasra at the end to show connection to the word إِذَا (when) that follows it
Adds 'and' at the beginning to connect it to what came before, and changes alif to a special form (ٱ) because it comes after و
This is the basic form of the word that means a specific point in time

steps.m: when (referring to something that will happen in the future)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end makes it refer specifically to future events. In this ayah, it refers to when the moon becomes full (ٱتَّسَقَ)

steps.m: to gather or join things together

steps.n: Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added 'it' and 'self' to show the moon gathering on its own (like becoming full)

steps.m: it became complete (said about القَمَرِ - the moon)
steps.n: The weak letter و combines with ت to make a doubled ت (shown by ّ). This word describes how the moon becomes full and complete.
Adding the fatha marks shows this is talking about something that happened in the past
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Added لَ at start to emphasize 'definitely/surely'

steps.m: you will most certainly ride (referring to stages humans will pass through)
steps.n: Added نَّ at end for extra emphasis. In this ayah, it's talking about how humans will definitely pass through different stages of life.

steps.m: to layer or stack one thing upon another

steps.n: Connected the letters together with fatha vowels to create a noun meaning one layer or stage

steps.m: a stage (object of لَتَرْكَبُنَّ - you will surely mount)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb لَتَرْكَبُنَّ (you will surely mount). This means you will go from one stage to another.

This is a preposition that shows movement from one state to another or comparison between things. In this ayah, it connects the word طَبَقًا (state/condition) to طَبَقٍ (another state/condition), showing movement or change from one to another.

steps.m: to stack or layer things on top of each other
a stage or state (that comes after طَبَقًا)
steps.n: Forms the noun meaning one complete layer or stage. This word follows طَبَقًا (stage) in the verse, showing movement from one state to another.
Added fatha to show this is the interrogative 'what' particle that asks questions

steps.m: Then what (connects to لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - they do not believe)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this question to the previous statement, showing consequence or result. This word introduces a question about why they don't believe.
Added fatha to show the basic vowel sound of the preposition
Added the pronoun هُمْ (them) to show who the preposition is referring to. This word connects to لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (they do not believe) in this ayah by showing who doesn't believe.
Basic negative particle that makes the verb after it negative, like adding 'not' in English. In this ayah, it makes 'they believe' into 'they do not believe'
Added fatha mark (َ) on ل to show how it's pronounced

steps.m: to trust, believe in, feel secure
steps.n: This is the basic past tense form of the verb
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from past tense (he believed) to present tense (he believes)

steps.m: they believe (in لَا context: they do not believe)
steps.n: Adds plural masculine ending to show multiple people doing the action. This word comes after لَا (not) in the ayah, so it means 'they do not believe'
The suffix shows that many people (all male or mixed group) are doing the action
This is a conditional particle that introduces a clause about when something happens. It's used here to describe what happens when the Quran is recited.
Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before it. This shows this is part of a sequence of descriptions.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: it was read/recited (referring to القُرْءَانُ - the Quran)
steps.n: Changed vowels to show that the Quran is what's being read, rather than showing who's reading it. This connects to القُرْءَانُ (the Quran) which comes later in the ayah.
Basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'. This word has no root since it's a preposition.
Changed the ending to prepare for attaching the pronoun. The ى changes to يْ when adding pronouns.
Added هِمُ which means 'them' (masculine plural). This word connects to لَا يَسْجُدُونَ (they do not prostrate) by showing who doesn't prostrate.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: recitation, that which is recited abundantly
steps.n: Changes the verb 'recite' into a noun meaning 'that which is recited' with emphasis on abundance
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ُ to show it's the subject of لَا يَسْجُدُونَ (they do not prostrate)
Basic negative particle that tells us the action in يَسْجُدُونَ (they prostrate) is not happening
Added fatha mark to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'

steps.m: to bow down with your forehead touching the ground
steps.n: Basic past tense form
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show it happens now, changed vowels to show ongoing action
Added ونَ at the end to show many people are doing it
This is a basic particle used to change direction in speech or correct a previous statement. In this ayah, it introduces a contrasting statement about those who disbelieve (ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟).
Added kasra at the end because it connects to the next word (ٱلَّذِينَ). This helps the words flow together smoothly when reading.
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific. The ل gets a shadda because it comes after ٱ.
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word connects to كَفَرُوا۟ (they disbelieved) to tell us who did the disbelieving.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb. This word describes the same people who are doing the denying (يُكَذِّبُونَ) in this ayah.
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people did this action. These are the same people who are denying (يُكَذِّبُونَ) in this ayah.

steps.m: to deny, to reject as false

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
The shadda (ّ) doubles the middle letter to show intensity or repetition of the action
Added يُ prefix and changed vowels to make it present tense

steps.m: they deny intensely/repeatedly (referring to those who disbelieved in previous ayah)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (those who disbelieved) in the previous part of the ayah.
This is a unique word that doesn't come from a standard root. It's the proper name of God in Arabic.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of أَعْلَمُ/knows best)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb أَعْلَمُ (knows best) in this ayah

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of أَعْلَمُ/knows best)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before, like saying 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to comparative/superlative form to show Allah's supreme knowledge. The ُ ending shows this describes Allah (اللَّهُ) in this ayah.
Starting with the basic relative pronoun ما which refers to things or actions

steps.m: with what/with whatever (they are keeping hidden in يُوعُونَ)
steps.n: Added the preposition بِ to show this is about doing something 'with' or 'by means of' what follows. In this ayah, it connects to يُوعُونَ (they are keeping hidden), showing Allah knows what they are concealing.
Changes the root letters into past tense verb meaning 'he retained/preserved'
Added يُ at the beginning to show present tense. The kasra under ع is needed because of the weak letter ي.
Added ون at the end to show multiple people doing the action. The ي at the end is dropped when adding ون.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The shadda doubles the middle letter to make the meaning stronger
Added فَ to show this is a result or consequence

steps.m: so give them news! (of عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ - a painful punishment)
steps.n: Added هُم to show who should receive the news. In this context, it's used sarcastically to announce bad news of punishment.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun that describes the action. This word is in the genitive case because it comes after the preposition ب (bi).
The word gets two dots under it (tanween) because it comes after a word like 'from' or 'with'

steps.m: with a punishment (connected to فَبَشِّرْهُم - then give them news)
steps.n: Added بِ which means 'with' or 'by'. This word is part of the phrase 'give them news with a punishment', showing what kind of news they will receive.
Added ي between second and third letters with vowels to make it into an adjective meaning 'causing intense pain'
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this is describing an indefinite noun (punishment)

This is the basic form of the exception particle that introduces what is excluded from a previous statement. In this ayah, it introduces those who believe and do good deeds as being excluded from loss.
Adding shadda and alif makes this a stronger exception particle. It's used here to exclude believers (الَّذِينَ) from those in loss.
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people

steps.m: those who (describing the believers in ءَامَنُوا۟)
steps.n: Added ال at the beginning with a shadda to make it specific. This word connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) to tell us which people we're talking about.
Adding alif after first letter changes meaning from just feeling safe to actively believing in something

steps.m: they believed (connected to وَعَمِلُوا۟ - and they did)
steps.n: Adding ُوا at the end changes 'he' to 'they'. This word connects to وَعَمِلُوا۟ (and they did) in the ayah, showing these are the same group of people doing both actions.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adds 'وا' at the end to make it 'they' instead of 'he' did

steps.m: and they did/worked (righteously) (connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ - believed)
steps.n: وَ means 'and' - it connects this action to believing in the previous word ءَامَنُوا۟
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who is righteous/good (active participle of صَلَحَ)
steps.n: This form shows someone or something characterized by righteousness/goodness
Changes the word to feminine plural form to refer to good actions or deeds rather than people
Makes the word definite to refer to specific righteous deeds mentioned in context (those done by believers)
Shows this word is the object of وَعَمِلُوا (they did/performed). The ِ ending shows it receives the action.
Added fatha to the letter lam to show it's connected to what follows
Added هُمْ (them) to show who the word 'for' refers to. This word connects to أَجْرٌ (reward) in the ayah, showing they have a reward.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a noun meaning the result of rewarding.
Added double damma to show this is indefinite (a/an) and is the subject in the phrase لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ (they have a reward).

steps.m: to change or be different from something

steps.n: Basic noun form showing something that is different or other than something else
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this word is describing the reward (أَجْرٌ) mentioned in the ayah. Both words have the same ending mark to show they go together.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to passive participle form showing something that is cut off/ended
Added tanween kasra to show this is describing the reward (أَجْرٌ) mentioned earlier in the ayah. The meaning becomes 'unending' or 'endless' when used with 'غَيْرُ' (not) before it.
